rag_rules: Fix AGREE diagnostics by resolving undefined references or removing invalid clauses instead of adding new architecture.
Do not introduce AADL property names like 'Period', 'Deadline', 'Priority' as AGREE variables; delete such clauses.
Ensure type consistency in AGREE expressions: numeric types must match expected scalar types (int, real, bool).
Validate that all identifiers used in AGREE clauses are visible in the current component scope.
generic_rules: When generating AGREE contracts, ensure all referenced identifiers are declared or visible in the same annex scope.
Avoid encoding AADL property associations directly into AGREE expressions unless they are explicitly typed and available.
Use only valid AADL feature names and types when constructing AGREE guarantees or assumptions.
If a reference cannot be resolved, remove the clause rather than attempt to define a new symbol.
syntax_or_pattern_reminders: AGREE expressions must maintain type consistency; e.g., int literals should not be assigned to bool variables.
AGREE annexes must be placed in appropriate AADL scopes: component types for assumptions/guarantees, implementations for bindings.
Helper functions and constants must be declared before use and match the expected scalar types (int, real, bool).
Do not assume that AADL property names like 'ms', 'Hz' are automatically available as AGREE identifiers.
anti_patterns: Do not attempt to define new AADL identifiers or properties in AGREE annexes that are not already present in the model.
Do not attempt to convert AADL property values (e.g., 'Period' or 'Deadline') into AGREE expressions without explicit model support.
Do not add new architecture elements or components when fixing AGREE diagnostics; only modify existing AGREE clauses.
irrelevant_topics: AGREE annex placement in component types vs. implementations
Model Analyst agent's role in extracting AADL structure
Requirement Analyst agent's role in traceability
Model Fusion agent's role in scope and owner preservation
